EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Sunday, July 14, another disturbance came around the low and further south. This caused showers and thunderstorms to evolve further south, and brought a large area of showers and thunderstorms to not only the Texas Hill Country, also across South Central Texas. Strong gusty winds accompanied some of the thunderstorms, with wind gusts of 35 to near 50 mph. Heavy rains in areas brought more localized flooding problems and caused rivers and streams to increase flow again. The showers and thunderstorms became numerous enough in the afternoon of Sunday the 14th to form a mesoscale boundary that moved southward across South Central Texas and to the coastal plains further south the night of the 14th and morning of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 3 inches of rain fell in just under 2 hours in the southern part of Kendall County near Boerne.  Brief flash flooding developed and continued through the mid afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were blown down on Highway 363.

With soils already saturated from rainfall on July 12 through 14, scattered thunderstorms re-developed near noon of July 15, in advance of a weak upper level system approaching from the southwest. Rainfall in an area from New Braunfels to Uvalde to Carrizo Springs to Cuero and back to New Braunfels was generally between 1 and 3 inches, with isolated totals of up to five inches.  Flash flooding began in the mid afternoon and continued into the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread 2 to 3 inch rainfall fell on Frio County through the early and mid afternoon.  Isolated areas received up to 5 inches of rain.  Flash flooding was reported during the early evening, then dissipated without significant damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon, the southeast section of Karnes County from Helena to Runge to Kenedy received between 3 and 4 inches of rainfall with some reports of up to 9 inches.  Flash flooding developed very quickly and continued until almost midnight.  Damage was generally confined to rural roads and a few low water crossings.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief flash flooding resulted from general rainfall of 1-2 inches, with up to 5 inches in the southeast part of the county.  Numerous roads were closed and additional evacuations were required in camps and vacation spots along the Frio River.
General 2 to 3 inch totals over Lee County with isolated amounts to 4 inches in the western part of the county caused low water crossings to flood briefly.  No damages were indicated.
Onion Creek crested at Highway 183 near Austin, at 23.8 feet on July 2 at 2100CST, where flood stage is 17.0 feet.

The repeated heavy rainfall produced general 3 to 4 inch rains over most of the counties from the Hill Country southward to US90.  Flash flooding began in the early morning hours and continued generally until the late morning.  Most low water crossings in the multi-county area were reported to be flooded and road closures were widespread.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: General 2 to 3 inch rain totals over the county, with isolated amounts of up to 4 inches in the south and west parts of the county, caused flash flooding over the entire area.  The flash flooding continued from around sunrise to the mid morning.  By the following day, flow into Lake Travis will bring it to its full elevation of 681 feet above Mean Sea Level.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: General 2 inch rainfall was reported over the county, with isolated totals to 4 inches in the south part of the county.  Flash flooding developed in the south part of the county before sunrise and continued until noon.  Damage to roads was reported to be minimal at this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms redeveloped over Wilson, Karnes, DeWitt and Lavaca Counties and began spreading slowly northward over already saturated soils.  The general 2 to 4 inch rainfalls had up to 7 inch totals embedded within them.  Again, no lives were lost in this part of the flooding, but damage to roads and bridges continued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: General 3 to 4 inch totals were reported across the county with isolated amounts of up to 7 inches over the western part of the county.  Flash flooding was occurring over all low water crossings.  Creeks and small streams were also reported flooding.

The general 2 to 4 inch rainfalls had up to 7 inch totals embedded within them.  Again, no lives were lost in this part of the flooding, but damage to roads and bridges continued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: General 2 to 3 inch totals fell over DeWitt County.  Isolated totals of up to 5 inches were indicated between Cuero and Westhoff.  Damage to roads was not indicated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved through most of western north Texas during the early morning of the 27th, with severe winds being reported across portions of Wichita, Archer, and Clay counties. This line of thunderstorms also moved through most of Hardeman, Foard, Knox, Wilbarger, and Baylor counties; however, winds were not severe.

The Medina River, already in flood since noon on the previous day, continue to build, and flash flooding was reported across all of the county.  Damages to roads and bridges continued to be reported.  All low water crossings were closed and travel was very hazardous.

Flash flooding began in the early morning hours and continued generally until the late morning.  Most low water crossings in the multi-county area were reported to be flooded and road closures were widespread.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: General 5 to 7 inch totals over the county, with isolated amounts as high as 12 inches, caused widespread flash flooding through the mid morning period over Kendall County.  Damage was becoming widespread to roads and bridges and travel was very hazardous.

These thunderstorms produced winds to 70 mph and hail up to golfball size.  The hardest hit areas were just to the west of Abilene and to the southeast of San Angelo.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Outflow from a severe thunderstorm gusted to 60 mph in northeast El Paso.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm moved southward into far west Texas and produced wind gusts as high as 80 mph which blew over a number of small water tanks and a mobile home. Quarter size hail also accompanied the storm, and flash flooding of two large arroyos left mud and debris on several roads in the area.

The tropical wave that has been producing heavy rains over South Central Texas peaked in intensity during the early morning hours of the 6th over the Big Country and Heartland Counties of West Central Texas. Numerous thunderstorms developed shortly after midnight across northern sections of Coleman and Brown Counties. into southern Callhan County, then spread west into the Abilene area around 3 AM.  Tropical rainfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour produced rainfall amounts of 10 to 14 inches directly over the Jim Ned Creek and Pecan Creek basins in northern Coleman and Brown Counties, and southern sections of Callahan and Taylor Counties. The Cities of Abilene and Brownwood received significant damage as the result of the flooding that followed the heavy rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rains of 10 to 12 inches fell over Coleman County in the early morning hours of the 6th.  This is in addition to the 8 to 10 inches that had fallen over the area in the days leading up to this flood. The area along Jim Ned Creek and Lake Coleman were hit especially hard. As the Coleman County police department was performing a water rescue on Jim Need Creek near Burkett, they were quoted saying, "a wall of water is flowing down Jim Need Creek."  Several roads and fences were damaged in the county, crops ruined and livestock missing.  In the Lake Coleman area, there were four homes with major damage and 2 homes with minor damage.
A rapidly moving line of thunderstorms produced wind gusts to 70 mph which resulted in roof damage and doors blown off a house east of Dell City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Friday, July 12, a large low pressure system settled over the southern Great Plains, sending a series of weather disturbances southeastward toward South Central Texas. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed in the afternoon and continued into the evening hours from the Hill Country eastward, producing locally heavy rain and beginning to saturate the soils still moist from the devastating rains earlier in July. Another disturbance arrived on Saturday, July 13, from the northwest, and came a little further south, bringing scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms to the Texas Hill Country and adjacent parts of South Central Texas on Saturday afternoon and evening of July 13th. These storms produced widespread 1 to 2 inch rain totals, with local amounts of up to 4 inches, causing brief flooding over several counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The northern part of Burnet County received 3 inches of rain in about 2 hours.  The area experienced flash flooding though the late afternoon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 3 inches of rain in just under 2 hours fell over northwestern Williamson County.  Flash flooding redeveloped quickly and continued through the late afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms struck the northwest parts of the Permian Basin during the early evening of the 1st.  The most intense storm was a supercell which moved from far northwest Andrews County across the western half of the Gaines County.  This storm produced very large hail and damaging winds.  Local newspaper reports indicate that crop and rural property damage was wide spread.  The most intense damage began about 17 miles west of Seminole, just south of U.S. Highway 180, and continued north.  Almost ninety power poles were blown down by the storm, and several thousand electrical customers were without power through the remainder of the night.  Crop damage was reported to be substaintial, with five circles of peanuts destroyed along with 8,000 acres of cotton.   Two additional severe storms moved across central Gaines County later in the evening.  These also produced large hail.  Close to 1,000 acres of cotton was also destroyed by these storms.The economical impact due to agricultural damage was reported to be significant, since crops could not be replanted so late in the growing season.  The total estimated agricultural losses are in the one to one and a half million dollar range.

Highway 66 and FM 35 flooded.

A stalled frontal boundary over south Texas in combination with a upper level storm system moving out of the Southwest Portion of the United States resulted in heavy rains and flooding for the second time this month across the Edwards Plateau and the Northwest Hill Country.  Rainfall amounts during the week of the 21st through the 25th were between 2 and 6 inches over much of the area. However, Crockett and Sutton Counties bore the brunt of the flooding with anywhere from 4 to 17 inches of rain.

Showers and thunderstorms spread northward from Wilson County into Guadalupe and Caldwell Counties just before midnight, with rain rates of up to 2 inches an hour.  Heavy rain was widespread across Caldwell County from just before midnight into the early morning hours, with general rain totals near 3 inches and scattered amounts of 5 to 7 inches in the southern part of the county.  Widespread flash flooding forced the closing of many county roads.

Widespread showers and thunderstorms, moving northward from Wilson County, covered most of Guadalupe County by 10 pm.  Rain falling at rates of up to 2 inches an hour began to produce countywide flash flooding before midnight and continuing to near sunrise.  General rain totals of 3 to 5 inches were indicated with widespread amounts of 6 to 8 inches over the county.  Severe flash flooding was reported by the Emergency Manager and the Guadalupe Sheriff's Department over the county.

The showers and thunderstorms continued to spread eastward through the midnight period.  Shortly after midnight, heavy rainfall brought flash flooding to the southern part of Bastrop County.  Rainfall rates were near 2 inches per hour and the rain continued to just past midnight.  Rain totals in the south part of the county were generally 2 to 3 inches with totals to 4 and 5 inches south of a Red Rock to Rosanky line.

Moving eastward into Fayette County from Bastrop County just prior to midnight, rainfall over the western half of the county averaged 2 to 3 inches.  Isolated totals of 6 to 8 were reported near LaGrange.  According to the Fayette County Sheriff's Department, flash flooding closed numerous county roads from just after midnight until nearly 3 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 6 and 8 inches of rain fell in less than 6 hours. Water filled up in underpasses up to 8 feet deep. A woman drove her car into the deep water, and drowned. A hospital had the emergency room floor flooded. Over 500 homes in Beaumont had water enter them.
Trees were blown down across Highway 73.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: As an upper level low pressure area approached from the west, scattered showers and thunderstorms in Atascosa County spread quickly into Wilson County.  The northeastward tracking storms dropped general 2 to 3 inch rain amounts over the northern part of Wilson County.  Isolated totals of up to 5 inches were indicated.  The Wilson County Sheriff's Department reported many roads closed due to flash flooding in the northern part of the county from late night into the early morning hours.

Rainfall over Maverick County was generally around 1 inch in the south and 2 inches in the north.  The heaviest rain fell near Quemado, where as much as 5 inches was reported.  Flash flooding began in the early morning hours and ended shortly after sunrise.  Damage was generally confined to roads in the north part of the County.

Showers and thunderstorms spread across Real County from Edwards County, with general rainfall of 1 to 2 inches countywide.  Isolated locations reported up to 4 inches.  Flash flooding closed most roads from early morning until nearly noon.  Damage was reported to area roads and bridges.

The storms reached Medina County near 2 am CST and continued into the mid morning.  Rainfall countywide averaged one inch, but isolated locations recorded as much as 3 inches.  Flash flooding began just before sunrise and ended just prior to lunch.  Minor damage was reported to roads and bridges.

Thunderstorms moved into Zavala County from Maverick County near 3 am CST and continued into the mid morning.  Average rainfall was between 1 and 2 inches, with isolated totals of up to 4 inches in the eastern part of the County.  Flash flooding began just before sunrise and ended just before noon.  Minor damage was reported to area roads and bridges.

Showers and thunderstorms moved into Atascosa County from the west just before sunrise and continued until near noon.  General rainfall amounts were between 1 and 2 inches, with widespread 3 inch amounts.  Heaviest amounts were near 5 inches in the extreme northern part of the County.  Damage was reported to be widespread over the roads and bridges in the northern part of the County.

Damage was reported to be widespread over the roads and bridges in the northern part of the County.

Heavy rain began in Bandera County near sunrise and continued into the mid-morning.  Rain totals were generally near 1 inch, with widespread 2 inch amounts and isolated totals to 4 inches in the northwest corner of the County.  Only minor damage was indicated to roads and bridges.

Minor damage was reported to roads and bridges.

No damage was indicated.

Scattered showers and thunderstorms began forming between Uvalde and Hondo just before midnight, with rain rates of up to 2 inches an hour.  These storms produced general rain amounts between 1 and 2 inches over southern Uvalde County, with isolated totals to 6 inches in the southeast corner of the county.  Flash flooding that began shortly after midnight ended prior to sunrise.
The Rio Grande River at Eagle Pass crested at 27.0 feet on October 9 at 800CST, where flood stage is 16.0 feet.
The Medina River crested at US 281 South at 28.1 feet on October 9 at 2045CST, where 20.0 feet is flood stage.
The San Antonio River crested at Elmendorf at 43.3 feet on October 9 at 2130CST, where 35.0 feet is flood stage.

Scattered showers and thunderstorms reformed by the mid morning in an area from LaPryor to Batesville to Frio Town.  These slow-moving showers and storms were producing rainfall at rates of an inch an hour, and accumulations by mid morning were approaching 3 inches.  Isolated totals near Frio Town eventually reached 5 inches.  The rain continued until just before noon, then ended.  With soils wet from recent rainfall, flash flooding began in the late morning and continued until near noon.

Scattered showers and thunderstorms redeveloping in the mid morning from LaPryor to Batesville to the Frio County line produced general rainfall of 2 to 4 inches with isolated totals in the Batesville area of 7 inches.  The rain continued from mid morning until just before noon.
The Guadalupe River at Gonzales crested at 40.7 feet on October 10 at 1500CST, where flood stage is 31.0 feet.
Cibolo Creek crested at Falls City at 26.9 feet on October 10 at 1515CST, where 17.0 feet is flood stage.

Near sunrise, the showers and thunderstorms spread into Dimmit County from Southern Maverick County.  Rainfall rates were not excessive, but a slow and steady rain finally produced general 1 to 2 inch amounts, with isolated totals in the eastern part of the County of up to 4 inches.  Brief flash flooding was indicated, with little or no damage.

By mid-morning, scattered showers and thunderstorms over Caldwell County had produced average rainfall of 1 to 2 inches, with isolated amounts of up to 3 inches, causing brief flash flooding.  No significant damage was reported.

A very brief but intense microburst stuck Cedar Park early on the morning of October 19.  The winds, estimated at between 80 and 90 mph blew down trees and power poles over a two-block area of the city.

Storms continued to near sunrise, with flash flooding developing quickly due to the saturated nature of the soils. General rainfall over Edwards County averaged 1 to 2 inches with up to 4 inches in isolated locations. Flash flooding developed in the early morning hours and ended just after sunrise.  Minor damage was reported to county roads.

Spotty, slow-moving showers formed in western Real County just prior to midnight and produced isolated totals up to 4 inches between Leakey and Barksdale.  Flash flooding began near 1 am that morning and continued for up to 3 hours.

A line of thunderstorms stalled just after midnight as it moved eastward across Bexar County, producing general 1 inch rainfall with isolated totals to 3 inches.  The city of San Antonio reported several rescues along US90 in the downtown area.  Numerous roads were closed across the city due to flash flooding through the early morning hours.

However, Crockett and Sutton Counties bore the brunt of the flooding with anywhere from 4 to 17 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains over the southern portions of Crockett county caused significant rises on the draws.  Several low water crossings were flooded.

Strong thunderstorm wind estimated at 80 mph blew over and destroyed a center pivot irrigation system, destroyed an unoccupied mobile home and a travel trailer, damaged a garage, and blew down a number of trees and power poles.
Wind-driven tennis ball size hail produced significant damage to at least 54 homes and a number of automobiles.  At least 2000 acres of cotton were destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary over south Texas in combination with a upper level storm system moving out of the Southwest Portion of the United States resulted in heavy rains and flooding for the second time this month across the Edwards Plateau and the Northwest Hill Country.  Rainfall amounts during the week of the 21st through the 25th were between 2 and 6 inches over much of the area. However, Crockett and Sutton Counties bore the brunt of the flooding with anywhere from 4 to 17 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Llano River flooded the South Llano State Park and flowed over Highway 377 for almost a day and a half. The bulk of the water came from heavy rains that fell on the upper end of the South Llano River in Northern Edwards County. There were several waves of heavy rains over southern Kimble County that produced several rises on the river as well and kept the river elevated for a long period of time.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds blew down power lines and rolled two trailer houses.  Damage extended from West Odessa to the city of Odessa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms struck the northwest parts of the Permian Basin during the early evening of the 1st.  The most intense storm was a supercell which moved from far northwest Andrews County across the western half of the Gaines County.  This storm produced very large hail and damaging winds.  Local newspaper reports indicate that crop and rural property damage was wide spread.  The most intense damage began about 17 miles west of Seminole, just south of U.S. Highway 180, and continued north.  Almost ninety power poles were blown down by the storm, and several thousand electrical customers were without power through the remainder of the night.  Crop damage was reported to be substaintial, with five circles of peanuts destroyed along with 8,000 acres of cotton.   Two additional severe storms moved across central Gaines County later in the evening.  These also produced large hail.  Close to 1,000 acres of cotton was also destroyed by these storms.The economical impact due to agricultural damage was reported to be significant, since crops could not be replanted so late in the growing season.  The total estimated agricultural losses are in the one to one and a half million dollar range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms moved into central and downtown El Paso from the south, dropping 1.5 to 2 inches of rain at the rate of an inch in 10 minutes on the southern spine of the Franklin Mountains. The excessive runoff resulted in several cars being washed off streets, and the closure of Interstate 10 for several hours. A child playing in flood waters was swept into a catchment basin. After being rescued, he died the following day. These storms also produced wet microbursts measured at 69 mph in two locations. An athletic dome still under construction at Cohen Stadium was severely damaged.

The afternoon and evening of the 13th brought an active severe weather situation to West Texas.  The primary severe weather mode was multicell storm structures with damaging winds.  However, a few storms exhibited supercellular characteristics, and one tornado was documented in western Reeves County.  Large hail to the size of golfballs was also reported.  The most significant damage reports were received from the West Odessa and Odessa areas in Ector County.  Winds up to 100 MPH were reported with the first  severe storm to impact that area, but those reports could not be substantiated by a NWS damage survey.  A second complex of storms affecting the area resulted in 70 MPH winds.  Damage was also reported from thunderstorm winds in Kermit and Fort Stockton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm wind gust of 64 MPH was measured at the Pecos Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Exactly one week after the December 23rd outbreak, Southeast Texas received another round of severe weather as the next storm system moved out of the southwestern U.S. toward Texas. This event only lasted three hours (compared to eighteen hours on the 23rd). This event only had three tornadoes (compared to ten on the 23rd). However, damage from two of these three tornadoes was significant due to the fact that it occurred over heavily populated areas. This event had no injuries, and the total damage cost was just under $450,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado briefly touched down before moving to the northeast into Galveston Bay as a waterspout. Several boats at the Houston Yacht Club were overturned and a large section of a metal roof was destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This December 23rd tornado and severe thunderstorm outbreak developed as a strong upper level low pressure system moved out of the southwestern U.S. and off to the east and northeast across the state of Texas. Abundant low level moisture was already in place across southeast Texas. Very strong shear, or turning and strengthening of the winds with height, was expected to last throughout the day. A warm front moved northward throughout the day and became a focus for the development of showers and thunderstorms. With the strong shear present, a significant number of thunderstorms quickly became severe. During an eighteen hour stretch, ten tornadoes and several large hail events were reported mainly north and west of the Houston area.  Locations from the Houston area to the beaches felt the brunt of this event in the evening when a squall line rapidly moved eastward toward the coast. Three injuries and over $800,000 in damage occurred with this outbreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: 60 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers had occurred over much of South Central Texas in advance of Tropical Storm Fay on Friday, Sept. 6. Tropical storm Fay came ashore near Palacios, near 6 am CST on Saturday, Sept. 7. Rainfall, at first spotty, became widespread by mid morning.  General ½ to 1 inch rains fell across the central parts of South Central Texas.  Heavier amounts had fallen from Bandera to San Antonio to Uvalde that averaged between 1 and 2 inches, with isolated totals to near 6 inches.  Flash flooding developed over Bandera, Bexar, Medina, and Uvalde Counties in the early afternoon, ending in the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: General 1 to 2 inch rainfall with isolated totals to 3 inches.  No damage was indicated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers had occurred over much of South Central Texas in advance of Tropical Storm Fay on Friday, Sept. 6. Tropical storm Fay came ashore near Palacios, near 6 am CST on Saturday, Sept. 7. Rainfall, at first spotty, became widespread by mid morning. Thunderstorms spread eastward from Karnes County into DeWitt County in the late morning.  Rainfall over the County averaged between 1 and 2 inches with widespread 3 inch amounts and isolated totals to 4 inches.  Flash flooding developed near noon and continued into the mid-afternoon.  Minor damage was reported to roads and bridges.

The storms began producing heavy rain in Fayette County in the late morning and continue until just past noon.  Rainfall amounts averaged 1 inch with widespread 2 inch amounts and isolated totals to 4 inches in the northeast part of the County.  Flash flooding began just after noon and ended by mid-afternoon.  No significant damage was reported.

Roads near I-45 across northern Galveston County flooded.
Golf ball size hail on West Airport Road.
The San Antonio River crested at Falls City at 18.3 feet on October 26 at 1245CST, where 12.0 feet is flood stage.
The Frio River at Frio crested at 9.4 feet on October 26 at 815CST, where flood stage is 6.0 feet.
The Guadalupe River at Cuero crested at 26.6 feet on October 28 at 1000CST, where flood stage is 20.0 feet.
The Nueces River at Asherton crested at 25.5 feet on October 28 at 2315CST, where flood stage is 20.0 feet.

By sunrise, showers and thunderstorms covered most of Frio County, with rain accumulations of 2 inches over the northeast sections.  The showers continued until nearly noon, with rain totals countywide of 1 to 2 inches and accumulations of up to 5 inches in the northeast part of the County.  Flash flooding began shortly after sunrise and ended shortly after noon.  Damages were mainly to roads and bridges.

By sunrise, showers and thunderstorms had been producing heavy rain over much of Kerr County for the past hour, and this continued until mid-morning.  Rainfall was heaviest in the western part of the County, where average amounts were 2 to 3 inches and isolated totals of up to 5 inches were reported.  Flash flooding began shortly after sunrise and continued until noon.  Damage was reported to County roads and bridges.
Numerous power lines and telephone lines downed. Roofs also blown off several businesses.
Flooding in and around Pearland (water within two feet of entering some homes).
Flooding occurring on Almeda and Beamer Roads. El Dorado Road under 1 to 2 feet of water.
Water on road

Several power poles were downed by strong thunderstorm winds, resulting in loss of power for residents in San Benito.

Showers and thunderstorms began in the late morning over Lavaca County and continued until mid-afternoon.  They produced general 1 inch amounts over the county, with isolated totals to 3 inches in the southwest corner.  Flash flooding began just after noon and ended in the mid-afternoon.

Heavy rain moved into Bastrop County from Travis and Caldwell Counties just before noon.  Rainfall amounts averaged 1 inch over the south part of the County, with isolated totals to 3 inches.  Brief flash flooding developed after noon and ended in the mid-afternoon.  No significant damage was indicated.
Cibolo Creek crested at Falls City at 22.2 feet on October 25 at 0115CST, where 17.0 feet is flood stage.
